content:
3 min read
Just now

--

I often see a substitution of concepts among those who share their experiences of interacting with AI. This happens because the language used to communicate with AI is too human and relatable.
To make a computer perform an action, you used to have to write code. First came machine code, then (using interpreter and compiler programs) code in a programming language that connected the programmer and the processor in a mutually understandable way.
Now, there is no need to learn a separate language. Think about it: first, we communicated with the computer in its own language (machine code, etc.); then came a compromise (compilers and interpreters allowed us to translate programming languages into machine code); and now, we use our own language.
Communicating with a computer and assigning tasks to it now happens in natural language, and in any language at that — whether Russian or English.
And that is the only thing that has changed. The computer still lacks intelligence, consciousness, emotional disposition, or judgment.
An unimaginable amount of information was fed into the neural network. All of it became “tokens” — units of information — and “vectors.” That is, the word “fire” became a certain set of numbers, a mathematically described pattern. And while a human, upon seeing this word, pictures a campfire, a fireplace, a match, a stove burner, heat, warmth, the feeling of their face burning, etc., the neural network sees only an associated dataset, not a real sensation, emotional evaluation, or any actual thoughts about “fire.”
This is the main problem when it comes to understanding interactions with AI and interpreting its answers. • Native, natural language of communication • A massive amount of data and an understanding of relevant words and associated concepts • A default setting to “help the human”. These factors combine to create an illusion — as if you are facing a thinking computer rather than a system based on mathematics and statistics.
The only way to communicate with a model is to realize how it works in order to eliminate these illusions. Every time you ask a question, you need to: 1️⃣ formulate the prompt (what is needed), 2️⃣ outline the knowledge base and provide possible data (instructions, PDF files, links, etc.), 3️⃣ set constraints (facts only from sources, 2–3 sentences, no descriptions, etc.).
The instructions described above create a framework for the AI, making the generation more accurate. Of course, AI often spices up its answers with phrases like “I understand you” or “Great idea” because it was trained on human responses — say, on hundreds of corporate emails featuring this kind of communication. But the point is that the AI does not “understand” you. The idea is not “great.” You read the text and mentally substitute “written correctly in my native language in response to my question” with “written by a thinking entity.” For the AI, every word in the prompt and in the response is math and statistics. Don’t fool yourself: work with the tool, but don’t consider it anything more than a tool.
I should add that agentic functions, like the use of tools, confuse people even more, but they work on the exact same principle. The fact that an AI can connect to something does not mean it understands its actions or its decisions. It executes commands using connected tools according to a plan it generated based on mathematics and statistics. There are no emotions, no feelings, and no “understanding” here.
